data.title: Douglas Elliman stock surges on report of takeover bid from Anywhere Real Estate
data.description:
Douglas Elliman (NYSE:DOUG) stock soared 33% in Friday morning trading after the real estate brokerage reportedly received a merger offer this week from one of its rivals that would value the business at almost double its unaffected share price.

Anywhere Real Estate (NYSE:HOUS), formerly called Realogy, approached Douglas Elliman (NYSE:DOUG) with a proposal valuing the company at more than $4 per share, Bloomberg News reported on Friday, citing a person familiar with the matter. That person said that Douglas Elliman was likely to reject the offer at its current level. On Thursday, Elliman stock closed at $2.14.

More than one person confirmed to Bloomberg that a proposal was made.

For Anywhere (NYSE:HOUS), the acquisition would boost its operations in large U.S. cities, such as New York and Miami, and would give it greater clout in luxury home sales. Anywhere Real Estate (HOUS) stock rose 4.5%.

The approach comes as Douglas Elliman (DOUG) fights lawsuits involving two of its former star agents. In December, Bloomberg reported that the Department of Justice requested information from the company about Oren and Tai Alexander, brothers and star agents that had worked for the brokerage. They were arrested that same month on drug and sexual assault charges.

In October, chairman and CEO Howard Lorber said he'd retire. Board member Michael Liebowitz was named to succeed him.

data.title: Roche wins EU endorsement for metastatic breast cancer treatment
data.description:
Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) (OTCQX:RHHBF) said that an advisory panel of the European Medicines Agency (EMA), endorsed its Itovebi combination for metastatic breast cancer treatment.

Issuing a so-called positive opinion, EMA’s Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (OTC:CHMP) has recommended the marketing authorization for Itovebi, in combination with palbociclib and fulvestrant, for adults with PIK3CA-mutated, ER-positive, HER2-negative advanced breast cancer that returned during or within a year after adjuvant hormone therapy.

The CHMP recommendation will next be reviewed by the European Commission (EC) before a final decision on marketing authorization, which is expected in the near future.

data.title: Movies push for holiday record with 'Lilo & Stitch,' eighth 'Mission: Impossible'
data.description:
A huge weekend is shaping up for the U.S. movie industry, with two major holiday-interval releases set to jump-start what had been a slow start to the film pipeline so far in 2025.

Memorial Day weekend is a traditional kick-off to summer movie season, the most lucrative time of the year for film exhibitors. And with the double dose of Tom Cruise in the eighth Mission: Impossible film (NASDAQ:PARA) (NASDAQ:PARAA) and Disney's live-action remake of its Lilo & Stitch (NYSE:DIS) over a three-day weekend, records may be set to fall.

The two films together should easily earn well over $200 million domestically, and together with solid May holdovers -- including Thunderbolts (NYSE:DIS), Final Destination: Bloodlines (NASDAQ:WBD) and even April's Sinners (NASDAQ:WBD), the weekend could challenge 2013's record Memorial Day industry domestic haul of $306M.

A sharp uptrend in most recent tracking suggests Lilo & Stitch (DIS) may be headed for $165M in a domestic opening, a number that would top Top Gun: Maverick's $160M for best Memorial Day opening ever.

Meanwhile, Mission: Impossible -- The Final Reckoning (NASDAQ:PARA) (NASDAQ:PARAA) is tracking toward some $75M-$85M over the long weekend, which would easily set an opening-tally record for the eight-film franchise.

Those two films will be followed in June by a quickening release pace, with Karate Kid: Legends (SONY) to come in a week; Ballerina (NYSE:LION), from the John Wick franchise, on June 6; and How to Train Your Dragon (NASDAQ:CMCSA) on June 13.

Analysts at Macquarie noted that with a strong film slate ahead, the first quarter is set to be the trough of 2025, with $1.4B in admissions revenues. They expect 2025 industry revenues to come in at $9B (down from a previous $9.2B estimate, thanks to a weak Q1), and noted the business' recession resistance, as well as a positive outlook at the CinemaCon convention.

One person who always publicly celebrates big release weekends is AMC (NYSE:AMC) CEO Adam Aron. "Memorial Day weekend is here, with movies at AMC like Lilo & Stitch, Mission: Impossible, Final Destination: Bloodlines, Sinners, Thunderbolts, The Accountant 2, A Minecraft Movie & more," he said on social media. "Anyone thinking this is going to be a really BIG movie-watching theatre-going weekend?"

Cinema tickers: AMC Entertainment (NYSE:AMC); Cinemark (NYSE:CNK); (NYSE:IMAX); Marcus (MCS); Reading International (RDI); Cineplex (CGX:CA); Alamo Drafthouse (SONY); National CineMedia (NCMI).

data.title: Deckers Outdoor bracing for tariff headwinds
data.description:
Deckers Outdoors (NYSE:DECK) is facing its steepest drop since January, with shares plunging over 20% on Friday after weak guidance and slowing sales expectations for its flagship HOKA brand puts the stock on track for its first weekly loss in five weeks

Although the company beat Q1 estimates and increased its buyback program, shares spiraled lower after-hours and continued to unravel during Friday’s regular session as investors zero in on the company’s warning over “demand erosion” and “general softness in the consumer spending environment.”

And despite mitigation efforts, Deckers (NYSE:DECK) expects “to absorb a portion of the tariff impact as these actions will not fully offset incremental costs in FY26,” CFO Steve Fasching said on the company’s earnings call, adding that the combination of price increases and general softness in the consumer spending environment will result in demand erosion.

Fasching expects an additional $150M in cost of goods sold in FY26 due to tariffs.

Besides flagging tariff headwinds, Q1 results showed HOKA sales missing Wall Street’s lofty estimates, suggesting that the brand’s explosive growth might be moderating at a time when the company is bracing for consumers to pump the brakes.

“We have yet to see the full impact of the tariff implementation,” Fasching added, as the impact from higher material costs, increased promotional pricing, and higher ocean freight rates will result in a decline in Deckers’ (NYSE:DECK) gross margin.

The sober outlook led Wall Street to temper their bullish view of the company, prompting Telsey Advisory to downgrade Deckers Outdoors (DECK) to Market Perform from Outperform, citing the deceleration of the direct-to-consumer and HOKA businesses.

Keybanc also downgraded Deckers (DECK) from Overweight to Sector Weight, citing HOKA sales as indicative the brand is “no longer as competitively positioned versus other disruptive running brands,” while Evercore’s downgrade to In-Line reflects a “new phase of lower growth profile.”

Footwear stocks are lower in sympathy with Deckers Outdoor (DECK) with shares of Nike (NKE), Crocs (CROX), Wolverine World Wide (WWW), On Holdings (ONON), Birkenstock (BIRK), adidas AG (OTCQX:ADDYY)(OTCQX:ADDDF), and Puma SE (OTCPK:PMMAF) all in the red.

data.title: Workday leads enterprise software stocks lower ahead of holiday weekend
data.description:
Enterprise software stocks were mostly lower on Friday, led by Workday (NASDAQ:WDAY), ahead of the Memorial Day weekend.

Workday shares fell 11% even as the back-office software provider reported first-quarter results and guidance that were mostly in line. The company reiterated second-quarter subscription revenue guidance at $2.16B (matching estimates) and raised its full-year adjusted operating margin forecast to 28.5% (up from 28%). Full-year subscription revenue is expected to reach $8.8B.

Autodesk shares fell fractionally, even as the construction software maker topped estimates for the first-quarter of fiscal 2026 and topped estimates for the coming periods. For the second-quarter, Autodesk guided revenue between $1.72B and $1.73B, surpassing the consensus of $1.7B, and adjusted earnings of $2.44–$2.48 per share, above the $2.34 estimate.

For the full year, Autodesk sees revenue of $6.92B–$6.99B, with the midpoint of $6.955B above the $6.93B consensus.

Palantir (PLTR) shares rose 0.5% after Wedbush Securities maintained its Outperform rating and spoke positively about the company's recent $795M contract with the U.S. Army.

“Palantir remains one of our top names to own in 2025 and this deal represents another opportunity for PLTR to capitalize on while continuing to generate unprecedented traction for its entire portfolio across in the federal and commercial landscapes,” said analysts led by Daniel Ives.

Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL) and Palo Alto Networks (PANW) were among the other enterprise software stocks seeing red on Friday amid renewed concerns about a trade war between the U.S. and Europe.

data.title: U.S. stocks slump after Trump threatens tariffs on Apple, EU
data.description:
U.S. stocks fell sharply on Friday, after President Donald Trump said tariffs must be paid on iPhones not made in the U.S. and also threatened a 50% tariff on the European Union.

The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) was last -1.0% in morning trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1.2%. The Dow (DJI) was -0.8%.

Trump, through his social media platform Truth Social, said he is recommending a straight 50% tariff on the European Union, commencing on June 1, 2025.

Trump also targeted Apple (AAPL), saying that the tech giant would have to pay 25% tariffs on iPhones not made in the U.S. Shares of the iPhone-maker were -2.8%.

U.S. Treasury yields continued to stabilize after a bruising bond sell-off on Wednesday. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 3 basis points to 4.51%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) also fell 3 basis points to 3.97%.   

Wall Street's major averages ended largely flat on Thursday. The focus remained on Trump's “One Big Beautiful” tax bill, which was narrowly passed by the U.S. House.

“After a heavy selloff on Wednesday, markets began to stabilize again over the last 24 hours, despite investors’ ongoing fears about the fiscal situation,” Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

One factor helping U.S. assets to stabilize was some stronger U.S. economic data, which continued to point away from a recession, Allen added.

Administration comments suggest Trump is willing to increase taxes on U.S. consumers of EU-made products if the EU does not make unilateral concessions on tariffs, UBS' Paul Donovan said.

“Markets are unlikely to place too much weight on these threats given the president’s tendency to rapidly retreat on such issues, but the situation does (again) increase policy uncertainty,” Donovan added.

The economic calendar is quiet today with mainly the April new home sales on tap. Economists on average expect it to fall to 694K.

U.S. markets will be closed on Monday for Memorial Day.

data.title: The bar for rate moves in any direction is higher, Chicago Fed's Goolsbee says
data.description:
Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee has set a higher bar for rate moves "in any direction" until more clarity emerges regarding trade policy and the economy, he said on Friday in a broadcast interview.

For the longer run, if the administration is putting in place higher tariffs that will have a stagflationary impact, "that's the central bank's worst situation," where both sides of the Federal Reserve's legal mandate worsen at the same time, he said during an interview on CNBC.

"We'll have to see how big the impacts on price are," Goolsbee said.

Imported good are only 11% of U.S. GDP, he noted. "If we don't go wild. If it doesn't jump out of its 11% lane, it could be like 2018 where we had tariffs directly affected impacted industries, but they didn't have a big macro effect."

Discussing recent rate increases at the long end of the bond markets, the market determines long rate conditions, Goolsbee said. "The Fed looks at the short end. To the extent that the conditions get tighter on the long end, that does affect investment decisions abnd consumer durables purchases directly."

He also discussed how businesses in his district feel about the shifts in trade policy.

"It's more than uncertainty. It's that non-consistency that for bigger decisions — the businesses are saying that they need to have some idea that it's going to be a constant that they can count on when they're going to make an investment," Goolsbee said.

He also provided context on the magnitude of tariffs that are being discussed. "To go to 10% was going to be the highest tariff rate that we had on the world in 90 years. To go to 50% is a completely different order of magnitude."

The district covered by the Chicago Fed is the most trade-exposed district of all. As businesses "look at tariff rates that are that high, that would be really scary for the supply chain," Goolsbee said.  At a Fed Listens event the Chicago Fed held last week, some businesses said the higher rates might be a "stepping stone" that leads to new trade deals, opening new markets for their products. But the unpredictability produces "a lot of anxiety" about potential supply chain disruptions, he said.

Currently, markets are forecasting the Fed's next rate cut at its September meeting, with a 50.6% probability of a 25 basis-point rate cut, and a 14.1% probability that the federal funds rate target range will be 50 bps lower than where it is now, according to the CME FedWatch tool.

The district central bank president also said he's "gratified" that the Supreme Court recognizes the importance of an independent Federal Reserve. On Thursday, the U.S.'s highest court exempted the country's central bank from the president removing its officials without cause.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Apple suppliers see red after Trump says tech giant must pay tariffs on iPhones not made in US
data.description:
Update: The story was updated with stock price movement.

Stocks of several suppliers of Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) fell on Friday after President Donald Trump said the tech giant would have to pay 25% tariffs on iPhones not made in the U.S.

Shares of Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) dipped about 1%, while Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) fell about 2%. Qorvo (NASDAQ:QRVO) and Skyworks Solutions (NASDAQ:SWKS) declined about 2% each. Qorvo and Skyworks make antenna tuners and radio frequency, or RF, related products for Apple. Meanwhile, Broadcom and Qualcomm provide chip related solutions used for the iPhone.

"I have long ago informed Tim Cook of Apple that I expect their iPhone’s that will be sold in the United States of America will be manufactured and built in the United States, not India, or anyplace else. If that is not the case, a Tariff of at least 25% must be paid by Apple to the U.S." Trump said in a post on social media platform Truth Social on Friday. 

Earlier this month, Trump had urged Cook to stop building facilities in India.

Apple's stock declined about 3%.

Jabil, which is a contract manufacturer of electrical components, was also in the red on Friday. However, shares of Hon Hai Precision (OTCPK:HNHAF) (OTCPK:HNHPF), also known as Foxconn Technology (OTCPK:FXCOF), rose about 1% in Taiwan. Earlier on Friday it was reported that Foxconn is among potential bidders for UTAC Holdings in a transaction that could value the Singapore-based semiconductor assembly and testing company at about $3B. 

Shares of Analog Devices (NASDAQ:ADI), another Apple supplier, declined about 1%.

data.title: Autolus Therapeutics gets EU endorsement for leukemia treatment
data.description:
Autolus Therapeutics (NASDAQ:AUTL) announced Friday that an expert panel of the EU drug regulator, the European Medicines Agency (EMA), endorsed obecabtagene autoleucel for leukemia treatment.

Issuing a so-called positive opinion, EMA’s Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) has recommended the marketing authorization for obecabtagene autoleucel for the treatment of adult patients, 26+, with relapsed or refractory B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia.

The CHMP recommendation will next be reviewed by the European Commission (EC) before a final decision on marketing authorization, which is expected within approximately two months.
